By the time we identified the fault, the system had been running in degraded mode for six hours.
The supplier had not completed the pre-commissioning checklist before we arrived.
The relay had been dropping out intermittently since the night before.
By Friday we will have verified all the interlocking tables.
João, we had an alarm on the ATP logic at 10:14, mid functional test. My team says it's just a glitch and we can carry on. Can we?

Not a glitch. By the time the alarm came up, the affected relay had been dropping out intermittently since the night before, the system had been running in degraded mode for about six hours. The alarm is the symptom; the intermittent contact is the cause.
All right, but is this a real defect, or one of the small snags we can just note and move past?

It's the defect, the failure mode is an intermittent contact on one relay. The loose label and the missing cable tag, those are snags for the punch list. But the interlocking had already fallen back to degraded mode as designed, so no unsafe command was issued, and the type approval stays valid, because it's an installation fault, not a design fault.
Fine. We'll swap the relay. Can my team keep testing that function in parallel to save the schedule?

I have to hold that one. Work will not resume on the affected function until the replacement relay has passed its own functional test. I'd like to flag this before we proceed, I'll log the non-conformance now and escalate it to the project manager.
Understood. And what do I tell my director about the witness test, where will we be by Friday?

Tell them it's a managed non-conformance, not a crisis. By the close of this shift we will have re-tested the ATP logic, and before the witness test on Friday we will have verified all the interlocking tables end to end. Four hours of downtime today, and the test back on track for Friday.
By the time we identified the fault, the system had been running in degraded mode for about six hours.
The supplier had not completed the pre-commissioning checklist before we arrived on site.
Before the witness test on Friday, we will have verified all the interlocking tables end to end.
That's the defect and the failure mode, an intermittent contact on a single trackside relay.
Work will not resume on that function until the replacement relay has passed its own functional test.

João, before you walk onto that factory floor for the witness test, let me tell you how I want you to report anything that goes wrong, because on site the language is the difference between a controlled finding and a panic. First, when a fault appears mid-test, do not narrate it in the simple past like a story. Reconstruct the sequence. Say what had already happened before you noticed the problem. If the system had been running in degraded mode for six hours before anyone flagged it, say exactly that, because the past perfect tells the supplier that the fault came first and the symptom came later, and that is the whole of root cause analysis. The engineer who says the relay failed after we had completed the pre-commissioning checklist sounds precise and in control. The one who just says the relay failed sounds like he is guessing. Second, name what you see with the right word. A defect is a defect, a snag is a small one you can clear later, a failure mode is the specific way the thing broke. Do not soften a defect into a problem or inflate a snag into a crisis. The supplier reads your vocabulary as your competence. Third, commit to the future with the future perfect. When the project director asks where you will be by Friday, do not say we will verify the tables, say we will have verified all the interlocking tables before the witness test on Friday. It tells everyone the deadline is real and the work will be finished, not merely started. And last, when you are not sure what someone said in a heavy accent, do not nod and hope. Ask. Can you walk me through this. Is this documented anywhere. Who has authority to sign this off. Repair the communication out loud, because a witness test is no place to guess. Read the fault, name it precisely, reconstruct what had happened, and commit clearly to what you will have done. That is field English under pressure.

Three rules for surviving a factory acceptance test in a language that is not your first, from someone who has stood on a lot of cold factory floors with a clipboard. First, slow the moment down before you speak. When a fault appears, the pressure makes you want to react instantly, and that is when your English collapses into the present tense and vague words. Take one breath and reconstruct the timeline. What had happened before the symptom showed. Had the equipment already passed the functional test when it failed, or had it been failing quietly the whole time. The past perfect is not decoration here, it is the tool that separates the cause from the effect, and on a witness test that distinction decides who is responsible. Second, protect your right to not understand. A supplier's engineer speaking fast, with a heavy accent, over the noise of a workshop, is hard for anyone. The weak move is to nod and pretend. The strong move is to ask, calmly, every time. Can you walk me through this. Is this documented anywhere. We need to put this on hold until we clarify. Asking is not weakness on site, it is exactly what a serious inspector does, and it protects you when the report is written later. Third, close every finding with a plan, not a worry. Do not leave a defect hanging in the air. Say what you will have done and by when. I will have logged the non-conformance today, and we will have re-run the functional test before the witness test on Friday. A defect with a deadline is a managed risk. A defect without one is a crisis. Reconstruct what had happened, ask until you are sure, and close with what you will have done. Do that, and you will run the floor in English as calmly as you run it in Portuguese.
